quiz.title: High-School Geometry: Unit 11 Quiz 12
quiz.subtitle: Comprehensive Mixed Calculation Practice
quiz.subject: Geometry
quiz.topic: Geometry: 11.12 Mixed Review — Surface Area and Volume

questions.question: A cube has side length $11$ cm. What is its volume?
questions.explanation: $V = s^3 = 11^3 = 1{,}331$ cm$^3$. The surface area would be $6 \times 11^2 = 726$ cm$^2$, but volume is about the space inside.

questions.question: A cylinder has radius $3.5$ m and height $12$ m. What is the lateral surface area, rounded to the nearest tenth?
questions.explanation: $LA = 2\pi rh = 2\pi(3.5)(12) = 84\pi \approx 263.89 \approx 263.9$ m$^2$. Lateral area is just the curved surface — it does not include the circular bases.

questions.question: A cone has radius $9$ cm and height $14$ cm. What is its volume in terms of $\pi$?
questions.explanation: $V = \frac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h = \frac{1}{3}\pi(81)(14) = \frac{1{,}134\pi}{3} = 378\pi$ cm$^3$. A cylinder with the same dimensions would have volume $1{,}134\pi$ cm$^3$ — exactly three times larger.

questions.question: A square pyramid has base side $10$ in and slant height $13$ in. What is its total surface area?
questions.explanation: Perimeter $P = 4 \times 10 = 40$ in. Lateral area $= \frac{1}{2}P\ell = \frac{1}{2} \times 40 \times 13 = 260$ in$^2$. Base $B = 10^2 = 100$ in$^2$. Total $= 260 + 100 = 360$ in$^2$.

questions.question: A sphere has diameter $14$ ft. What is its volume, rounded to the nearest tenth?
questions.explanation: Radius $= 7$ ft. $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi(7^3) = \frac{4}{3}\pi(343) = \frac{1{,}372\pi}{3} \approx 1{,}436.76 \approx 1{,}436.8$ ft$^3$. When given diameter, always find the radius first.

questions.question: A cylinder has radius $5$ cm and height $9$ cm. What is the total surface area in terms of $\pi$?
questions.explanation: Lateral $= 2\pi(5)(9) = 90\pi$ cm$^2$. Two bases $= 2\pi(5^2) = 50\pi$ cm$^2$. Total $= 90\pi + 50\pi = 140\pi$ cm$^2$. Surface area covers the curved side plus both circular ends.

questions.question: Two similar rectangular prisms have volumes in the ratio $27:64$. The smaller prism has surface area $135$ cm$^2$. What is the larger prism's surface area?
questions.explanation: $k^3 = \frac{64}{27}$, so $k = \frac{4}{3}$. $k^2 = (\frac{4}{3})^2 = \frac{16}{9}$. $SA_{\text{large}} = 135 \times \frac{16}{9} = 15 \times 16 = 240$ cm$^2$. From volume ratio, find $k$ via cube root, then square for surface area ratio.

questions.question: A square pyramid is carved out of a cube of side $12$ cm. The pyramid's base matches one face of the cube, and its vertex reaches the center of the opposite face. What is the remaining volume?
questions.explanation: Cube: $V = 12^3 = 1{,}728$ cm$^3$. Pyramid: $V = \frac{1}{3} \times 12^2 \times 12 = \frac{1}{3} \times 1{,}728 = 576$ cm$^3$. Remaining $= 1{,}728 - 576 = 1{,}152$ cm$^3$. The pyramid occupies exactly one-third of the cube.

questions.question: A platinum bar has mass $2{,}145$ grams. Platinum has density $21.45$ g/cm$^3$. What is the bar's volume?
questions.explanation: $V = \frac{m}{D} = \frac{2{,}145}{21.45} = 100$ cm$^3$. This is a cube about $4.64$ cm on each side. Platinum is very dense, so a relatively small volume has significant mass.

questions.question: A cylindrical tank of radius $6$ ft and height $10$ ft is open at the top and has a hemispherical bottom (replacing the flat base). What is the total exterior surface area, rounded to the nearest tenth?
questions.explanation: Cylinder lateral $= 2\pi(6)(10) = 120\pi$. Top is open — no top base. Hemispherical bottom curved surface $= 2\pi(6^2) = 72\pi$. Total $= 120\pi + 72\pi = 192\pi \approx 603.19 \approx 603.2$ ft$^2$. The flat bottom is replaced by the curved hemisphere, which has twice the area of the original flat base.

scoreMessages.90: Outstanding! You have mastered the full range of Unit 11: volume of cubes, cylinders, cones, pyramids, and spheres; surface area of cylinders, pyramids, and composite solids; similar solids scaling; density calculations; and real-world applications.
scoreMessages.70: Good work across the unit! Review any missed topics: (1) Cone volume: $\frac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h$, (2) Pyramid SA uses slant height $\ell$, (3) Sphere volume uses radius, not diameter, (4) For similar solids, $k = \sqrt[3]{\frac{V_1}{V_2}}$, (5) For composite SA, identify and subtract hidden faces.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start on this mixed review. Key reminders: (1) $V_{\text{sphere}} = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$, (2) $SA_{\	ext{cylinder}} = 2\pi rh + 2\pi r^2$, (3) Pyramid occupies $\frac{1}{3}$ of its bounding prism, (4) $V = \frac{m}{D}$ rearranges the density formula, (5) Hemispherical surfaces replace flat surfaces in some designs.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! This review spans the unit: prisms/cylinders ($V = Bh$, $SA = Ph + 2B$), pyramids/cones ($V = \frac{1}{3}Bh$), spheres ($V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$, $SA = 4\pi r^2$), composite solids (add volumes, subtract hidden SA), similar solids ($k^2$ for area, $k^3$ for volume), and density ($m = DV$).
